title: Developmental Disabilities — ID Levels, Genetic Conditions & Services (Set 2)
subtitle: Intellectual disability, syndromes, health maintenance, regional centers & rights
scenario: Understanding intellectual disability, common genetic and metabolic conditions, health maintenance, and the California service system for people with developmental disabilities.

steps.q: Most people with an intellectual disability fall into which severity level, and are able to gain considerable independence with support?

opts.rat: The majority of people with intellectual disability have MILD severity and, with appropriate support, can achieve substantial independence in daily living and work.
Profound intellectual disability is the least common and involves the greatest support needs.
Severe intellectual disability is less common and involves extensive support needs.
Intellectual disability is described by severity levels based on adaptive functioning.

steps.q: Severity of intellectual disability is determined primarily by the person's:

opts.rat: Age does not determine the severity level.

opts.text: Adaptive functioning across conceptual, social, and practical skills
opts.rat: Current criteria base severity mainly on adaptive functioning (conceptual, social, and practical domains), not IQ score alone.
Physical size is unrelated to severity classification.
This is irrelevant to intellectual disability severity.

steps.q: Which condition is the MOST common inherited cause of intellectual disability?

opts.rat: This is not a cause of intellectual disability.
Allergies do not cause intellectual disability.
Fragile X syndrome is the most common inherited (genetic) cause of intellectual disability.
Refractive error is unrelated to intellectual disability.

steps.q: A client with Down syndrome is admitted. The technician knows this condition is commonly associated with:

opts.rat: People with Down syndrome are not immune to infection; they may be more susceptible.
Short stature, not tall, is typical.
This is not a feature of Down syndrome.
Congenital heart defects occur in a large proportion of people with Down syndrome and require monitoring.

steps.q: Clients with Down syndrome should be periodically screened for which common endocrine problem?

opts.rat: Hypothyroidism is common in Down syndrome, so periodic thyroid screening is recommended.
This is not associated with Down syndrome.
This is not a typical concern in Down syndrome.
Thyroid disease is notably more common in Down syndrome.

steps.q: A client with Prader-Willi syndrome has an insatiable appetite (hyperphagia). A key management strategy is to:

opts.rat: Unlimited food access is dangerous in Prader-Willi syndrome.

opts.text: Supervise and limit access to food to prevent life-threatening overeating
opts.rat: Prader-Willi syndrome causes uncontrolled appetite; structured food access and supervision are essential to prevent dangerous obesity and complications.
The overeating must be actively managed for safety.
Withholding meals is harmful; structured, controlled portions are used.

steps.q: A client with phenylketonuria (PKU) requires which dietary management?

opts.rat: High protein provides phenylalanine, which is harmful in PKU.
Aspartame contains phenylalanine and must be avoided.

opts.text: A diet low in phenylalanine, avoiding aspartame and high-protein foods
opts.rat: PKU requires restricting phenylalanine; aspartame and high-protein foods contain phenylalanine and must be limited to prevent neurologic damage.
Dietary management is essential in PKU.

steps.q: Why is EARLY intervention important for a child with a developmental disability?

opts.rat: Early intervention improves outcomes but does not cure a developmental disability.

opts.text: It is only useful after adulthood
opts.rat: Intervention is most effective early, not delayed to adulthood.
Early intervention has well-documented benefits.

opts.text: It maximizes developmental progress and functional outcomes
opts.rat: Early intervention takes advantage of developmental plasticity and improves long-term functional outcomes.

steps.q: To promote independence for a client with a developmental disability, the technician should:

opts.text: Provide adaptive equipment and support the client in doing tasks themselves
opts.rat: Adaptive equipment and supported practice let the client perform tasks as independently as possible, promoting dignity and skill.

opts.text: Complete all tasks for the client
opts.rat: Doing everything for the client fosters dependence.

opts.text: Discourage the client from trying new skills
opts.rat: Discouraging skill-building limits growth and independence.

opts.text: Remove all choices from daily routines
opts.rat: Removing choice undermines self-determination.

steps.q: In California, which agencies coordinate and fund services for people with developmental disabilities?

opts.rat: Fire departments do not coordinate developmental-disability services.
California regional centers coordinate assessment, planning, and services for people with developmental disabilities.
Libraries are not the service-coordination agency.
This is unrelated to developmental-disability services.

steps.q: The Individual Program Plan (IPP) for a client with a developmental disability should be:

opts.text: Written by one staff member without any input
opts.rat: The plan must involve the individual and team, not be made unilaterally.
Plans are individualized, not standardized.

opts.text: Person-centered and developed with the client and their family/team
opts.rat: The IPP is a person-centered plan created with the individual and their support team to reflect their goals and needs.
The individual participates in and knows their own plan.

steps.q: According to the least-restrictive-environment principle, an adult with a mild developmental disability who can live with support should ideally reside in:

opts.text: A locked institution regardless of ability
opts.rat: Institutionalizing someone who can live in the community violates least-restrictive principles.
Community integration, not isolation, is the goal.
Indefinite hospitalization is not appropriate for stable community living needs.

opts.text: A community setting such as a group home or supported apartment
opts.rat: Least-restrictive care favors community living (e.g., group homes, supported apartments) over institutionalization when the person can be safely supported there.

steps.q: When supporting an adult with a developmental disability in making decisions, the BEST approach is to:

opts.text: Offer information in an understandable way and support the person's own choices
opts.rat: Supported decision-making provides accessible information and honors the person's right to make their own choices whenever possible.

opts.text: Make all decisions for them without asking
opts.rat: Making all decisions removes autonomy and self-determination.
Assuming incapacity is disrespectful and often inaccurate.

opts.text: Pressure them into the staff's preferred choice
opts.rat: Coercion violates the person's rights.

steps.q: People with developmental disabilities often have unmet health needs. The technician should promote:

opts.text: Care only when a crisis occurs
opts.rat: Waiting for crises leads to preventable complications.

opts.text: Routine preventive care and regular health screenings
opts.rat: People with developmental disabilities are at risk for overlooked health problems; routine preventive care and screenings help catch issues early.
Avoiding care worsens health outcomes.
Preventive services including vaccines and dental care are important.

steps.q: Dental care deserves special attention in clients with developmental disabilities because they:

opts.rat: They are not immune to dental disease; risk is often higher.
Oral care is essential for all clients.

opts.text: Often have higher rates of oral health problems and may need extra support with hygiene
opts.rat: Clients with developmental disabilities frequently have more dental/oral health problems and may need assistance and adapted approaches for oral hygiene.
Avoiding brushing worsens oral health.

steps.q: As adults with developmental disabilities age, the technician should be aware that they may:

opts.text: Become immune to all illness with age
opts.rat: Aging does not confer immunity to illness.

opts.text: No longer require any health monitoring
opts.rat: Ongoing monitoring remains important.

opts.text: Reverse their disability as they age
opts.rat: Developmental disabilities are lifelong.

opts.text: Experience some age-related health changes earlier than the general population
opts.rat: Some adults with developmental disabilities experience age-related conditions earlier, so monitoring for changes in health and function is important.

steps.q: Promoting self-determination for a client with a developmental disability means:

opts.text: Offering meaningful choices and respecting the client's preferences
opts.rat: Self-determination means supporting the person to make choices about their own life and honoring their preferences.
Removing choice is the opposite of self-determination.

opts.text: Making every decision for the client
opts.rat: Deciding everything for the client removes autonomy.
Ignoring wishes disrespects the person's rights.

steps.q: Supported employment for adults with developmental disabilities aims to:

opts.text: Keep the person from ever working
opts.rat: The goal is to enable, not prevent, employment.

opts.text: Help the person work in the community with individualized supports
opts.rat: Supported employment provides individualized assistance so the person can work successfully in a community/integrated setting.
Integration with coworkers is a goal, not isolation.
The model specifically provides individualized supports.

steps.q: Regarding relationships and sexuality, adults with developmental disabilities have the right to:

opts.text: No information on the topic at all
opts.rat: Withholding all education is not respectful of their rights or safety.

opts.text: Be treated as if they have no feelings
opts.rat: This is disrespectful and inaccurate.

opts.text: Age-appropriate education and respect for their rights, with support for safety
opts.rat: Adults with developmental disabilities have rights to relationships and to appropriate education, delivered with respect and attention to safety and consent.
Punishing normal expression violates their rights.

steps.q: The exhausted family caregiver of an adult with a developmental disability could BEST be supported by:

opts.text: Telling the caregiver to simply cope alone
opts.rat: Dismissing the caregiver's needs is not supportive.
Outside support is beneficial, not something to discourage.
Caregiver burnout should be recognized and addressed.

opts.text: Sharing information about respite care and support resources with the team
opts.rat: Connecting families to respite care and support resources helps sustain caregivers and benefits the client.
